docs.title: Abbreviations
docs.text:
Quick reference for the abbreviations, acronyms, and unit symbols used throughout the compendium. Linked entries point to the page where the term is defined or used in detail.

  • 2-PAM - Pralidoxime (nerve-agent antidote). See Medical.
  • AC - Armor Class. See Armor.
  • ACH - Advanced Combat Helmet. See Armor: Helmets.
  • ACP - Automatic Colt Pistol (cartridge family, e.g. .45 ACP). See Pistols.
  • APRS - Automatic Packet Reporting System. See Comms.
  • APT - Advanced Persistent Threat.
  • AR - Assault Rifle. See Assault Rifles.
  • AVS - Adaptive Vest System. See Armor.
  • BAS - Battalion Aid Station (advanced equipment access). See Combat Medic.
  • BMG - Browning Machine Gun (e.g. .50 BMG). See Machine Guns.
  • BVM - Bag-Valve Mask. See Medical.
  • C4 - Composition 4 (plastic explosive). See Demolitions.
  • CANA - Convulsant Antidote, Nerve Agent (diazepam auto-injector). See Medical.
  • CAT - Combat Application Tourniquet. See Medical.
  • CBRN - Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear. See Medical.
  • CHA - Charisma. See Attributes.
  • cm - Centimeter.
  • CON - Constitution. See Attributes.
  • COTS - Commercial Off-The-Shelf. See Drones.
  • CQB - Close Quarters Battle. See Martial Weapon Programs.
  • CQC - Close Quarter Combat (weapon property). See Weapon Properties.
  • CS - Tear gas (2-chlorobenzalmalononitrile). See Saving Throws.
  • CSAR - Combat Search and Rescue.
  • CSI - Crime Scene Investigation.
  • D&D - Dungeons & Dragons.
  • DAPS - Deltoid/Axillary Protective System. See Armor: Add-on Soft Armor.
  • DC - Difficulty Class. See Ability Checks.
  • DEX - Dexterity. See Attributes.
  • DMR - Designated Marksman Rifle. See Semi-Auto Rifles.
  • DOPE - Data On Previous Engagements (marksman's data book). See Gear.
  • DR - Damage Reduction. See Damage Types.
  • ECWCS - Extreme Cold Weather Clothing System. See Clothing.
  • EKG - Electrocardiogram. See Medical.
  • EOD - Explosive Ordnance Disposal. See Demolitions Specialist.
  • ESAPI - Enhanced Small Arms Protective Insert (plate). See Armor: Plates.
  • ET - Endotracheal (airway). See Medical.
  • FMJ - Full Metal Jacket (ammunition type). See Armor: Stopping Power.
  • FOV - Field of View. See Optics.
  • FRS - Family Radio Service. See Comms.
  • ft - Feet. See Movement.
  • FUBAR - Fouled Up Beyond All Recognition (weapon malfunction). See Weapon Properties.
  • GPMG - General Purpose Machine Gun. See Machine Guns.
  • GPS - Global Positioning System. See Field Kit.
  • gr - Grain (bullet mass).
  • HF - High Frequency (radio band). See Comms.
  • HMG - Heavy Machine Gun. See Machine Guns.
  • HP - Hit Points. See Hit Points.
  • hr - Hour.
  • HVT - High-Value Target.
  • ICS - Industrial Control Systems.
  • IED - Improvised Explosive Device.
  • IFAK - Individual First Aid Kit. See Medical.
  • IFF - Identification Friend or Foe.
  • in - Inch.
  • INT - Intelligence. See Attributes.
  • IR - Infrared. See Optics.
  • IV - Intravenous. See Medical.
  • JPC - Jumpable Plate Carrier. See Armor.
  • JTAC - Joint Tactical Air Controller.
  • JTRS - Joint Tactical Radio System. See Comms.
  • kg - Kilogram. See Encumbrance.
  • KI - Potassium Iodide. See Medical.
  • lb - Pound. See Encumbrance.
  • LBS - Load-Bearing System (vest/rig).
  • LMG - Light Machine Gun. See Machine Guns.
  • LoRa - Long Range (mesh radio protocol). See Comms.
  • LSW - Light Support Weapon. See Machine Guns.
  • m - Meter.
  • MAG - Magazine (ammunition container).
  • MBITR - Multiband Inter/Intra Team Radio. See Comms.
  • MICH - Modular Integrated Communications Helmet. See Armor: Helmets.
  • min - Minute.
  • mm - Millimeter.
  • MOA - Minute of Angle (angular accuracy).
  • MOLLE - Modular Lightweight Load-carrying Equipment. See Packs.
  • NATO - North Atlantic Treaty Organization.
  • NIJ - National Institute of Justice (armor rating standard, levels IIA / II / IIIA / III / III+ / IV). See Armor: Stopping Power.
  • NPC - Non-Player Character.
  • NVG - Night Vision Goggles. See Optics: Night Vision.
  • ODT - Oral Dissolvable Tablet. See Medical.
  • OPA - Oropharyngeal Airway. See Medical.
  • OT - Operational Technology.
  • OTFC - Oral Transmucosal Fentanyl Citrate. See Medical.
  • oz - Ounce.
  • P - Parabellum (e.g. 9mm P). See Pistols.
  • PASGT - Personnel Armor System for Ground Troops (helmet). See Armor: Helmets.
  • PBX - Private Branch Exchange (phone system). See Comms.
  • PLRF - Pulsed Laser Rangefinder. See Optics.
  • PPE - Personal Protective Equipment.
  • PTT - Push-to-Talk. See Comms.
  • QRP - Low-power radio operation. See Comms.
  • RF - Radio Frequency.
  • RFID - Radio Frequency Identification.
  • RPG - Rocket Propelled Grenade. See Rocket Launchers.
  • S/B/A - Single / Burst / Auto fire selector modes. See Weapon Properties.
  • SAM - Junctional tourniquet (brand). See Medical.
  • SAPI - Small Arms Protective Insert (plate). See Armor: Plates.
  • SAR - Search and Rescue.
  • SAW - Squad Automatic Weapon. See Machine Guns.
  • sec - Second.
  • SERE - Survival, Evasion, Resistance, Escape. See Gear.
  • SMG - Submachine Gun. See Submachine Guns.
  • SOF - Special Operations Forces.
  • SOF-T - Special Operations Forces Tourniquet. See Medical.
  • STR - Strength. See Attributes.
  • T&E - Traverse and Elevation (machine gun mount mechanism). See Gear.
  • TBI - Traumatic Brain Injury. See Medical.
  • TCCC - Tactical Combat Casualty Care. See Medical.
  • TXA - Tranexamic Acid (antifibrinolytic). See Medical.
  • UHF - Ultra High Frequency. See Comms.
  • UV - Ultraviolet.
  • VHF - Very High Frequency. See Comms.
  • VS-17 - Visual signal panel. See Comms.
  • WIS - Wisdom. See Attributes.

docs.title: Armor and Protective Gear
docs.text: For protective gloves and boots, see Clothing - Gloves and Clothing - Footwear.

docs.title: Stopping Power (NIJ Levels)
docs.text:
Armor in this compendium is rated by the NIJ standard. The Stops column on every table below refers to the soft panel's rating; rifle plate stopping power is listed separately under Plates.

Level Stops IIA 9mm, .40 S&W (subsonic pistol). II 9mm +P, .357 Magnum (standard pistol). IIIA .357 SIG, .44 Magnum, most handguns and shotgun slugs (heavy soft). III 7.62×51 FMJ and most standard rifle rounds. III+ + 5.56 M855 and 7.62×39 MSC "green tip" steel-core. IV .30-06 AP and most armor-piercing rifle rounds.

AC bonus and DR from armor/plates apply only to the side/slot they cover. The GM adjudicates which piece catches a given hit.

docs.title: Covert (Concealable)
docs.text:
Worn under civilian clothing for grayman work, protective details, and undercover ops. No plate pockets.

Vest AC Stops Modifiers Resistances Coverage Weight Req Concealable Vest (IIA) 11 + DEX IIA - Piercing Chest, Back 1.5 kg 3 Concealable Vest (IIIA) 12 + DEX IIIA - Piercing, Slashing Chest, Back 2.5 kg 5 Safariland SX01 Elite 12 + DEX IIIA - Piercing, Slashing Chest, Back 2.2 kg 8 Point Blank Vision 12 + DEX IIIA - Piercing, Slashing Chest, Back 2.3 kg 8

docs.title: Plate Carriers
docs.text:
Modular shells that accept rifle plates. Stops refers to optional soft-armor inserts; plate stopping power comes from the plates themselves (see Plates).

Carrier AC Stops Modifiers Stealth Resistances Coverage Plate Pockets Weight Req Plate Carrier (Light) 12 + DEX - - - Piercing, Slashing Torso Front/Back 5.5 kg 5 Crye JPC 12 + DEX - - - Piercing, Slashing Torso Front/Back 5.0 kg 10 Crye AVS 13 + DEX (max 3) IIIA - - Piercing, Slashing Torso + Sides Front/Back + Side × 2 7.0 kg 15 LBT-6094 13 + DEX (max 3) IIIA - - Piercing, Slashing Torso + Sides Front/Back + Side × 2 7.5 kg 15 Eagle CIRAS 13 + DEX (max 3) IIIA - - Piercing, Slashing Torso + Sides Front/Back + Side × 2 8.0 kg 15

docs.title: Flak Vests and Body Armor
docs.text:
Soft armor with integrated frag protection and plate pockets.

Vest AC Stops Modifiers Stealth Resistances Coverage Plate Pockets Weight Req Flak Jacket (Light) 13 + DEX (max 3) IIIA - - Piercing, Slashing Chest, Back, Groin Torso + Side 7.5 kg 10 Medium Flak / IBA 14 + DEX (max 2) IIIA - Disadvantage Piercing, Slashing Chest, Back, Groin Torso F/B, Side × 2 12 kg 15 Medium Flak w/ Shoulders 15 + DEX (max 2) IIIA - Disadvantage Piercing, Slashing Chest, Back, Arms, Groin Torso F/B, Side × 2 14.5 kg 20 Medium Flak w/ Shoulders & Legs 16 IIIA Disadvantage on DEX saves Disadvantage Piercing, Slashing Chest, Back, Arms, Legs, Groin Torso F/B, Side × 2 14.5 kg 25 Bomb Suit (EOD) 18 Frag/IV -10 ft movement, disadvantage on DEX saves and checks Disadvantage Bludgeoning, Piercing, Slashing, Force Chest, Back, Arms, Legs, Groin Torso F/B, Side 40 kg 40

docs.title: Plates
docs.text: Rifle plates slot into carrier and vest pockets. A plate only protects the side it's installed on.

docs.title: Torso Plates
docs.text: Plate Level Stops Benefit Weight (each) Req Steel Plate (III) III 7.62×51 FMJ and standard rifle rounds 2 DR vs shots to the covered side 4.0 kg 5 Steel Plate (III+) III+ + 5.56 M855, 7.62×39 MSC green tip 2 DR vs shots to the covered side 4.5 kg 8 Ceramic SAPI (III) III 7.62×51 FMJ and standard rifle rounds 2 DR vs shots to the covered side 2.5 kg 10 Ceramic ESAPI (IV) IV .30-06 AP and most rifle rounds 3 DR vs shots to the covered side 2.8 kg 15 Polyethylene (III) III 7.62×51 FMJ and standard rifle rounds 2 DR vs shots to the covered side 1.5 kg 20 Polyethylene (III+) III+ + 5.56 M855, 7.62×39 MSC green tip 2 DR vs shots to the covered side 1.8 kg 25

docs.title: Positional Plates
docs.text:
Smaller plates for flanks, shoulders, throat, groin, and legs. Stats are for Ceramic SAPI (III) by default; equivalent material substitutions follow the same weight/Req deltas as Torso Plates.

Plate Coverage Benefit Weight (each) Req Side Plate Flank (under arm) 2 DR vs shots to the covered flank 1.5 kg 10 Shoulder Plate Rear torso / shoulders 2 DR vs rear torso attacks 0.5 kg 10 Leg Plates (pair) Upper legs / groin 2 DR from both plates vs torso/groin shots 3.0 kg 10 Throat Plate Neck / throat 2 DR vs neck attacks 0.3 kg 8 Groin Plate (hard) Groin 2 DR vs groin attacks 0.5 kg 8

docs.title: Helmets
docs.text: Helmet AC Stops Modifiers Resistances Weight Req Bump Helmet (Ops-Core) +1 - - Bludgeoning, Slashing 0.6 kg 3 Tactical Helmet +1 - - Bludgeoning, Slashing 0.9 kg 5 PASGT +1 IIIA -2 Perception Piercing, Slashing, Bludgeoning 1.6 kg 8 MICH / ACH +1 IIIA -1 Perception Piercing, Slashing, Bludgeoning 1.5 kg 10 Ops-Core FAST Ballistic +1 IIIA - Piercing, Slashing, Bludgeoning 1.3 kg 15 Team Wendy Exfil Ballistic +1 IIIA - Piercing, Slashing, Bludgeoning 1.3 kg 15 Crye AirFrame +1 IIIA - Piercing, Slashing, Bludgeoning 1.2 kg 18 Bomb Suit Helm +2 - -5 Perception Force, Piercing, Slashing, Bludgeoning 1.8 kg 20

docs.text:
Additional soft-armor pieces that attach to a carrier or vest.

Piece Stops Effect Coverage Weight Req DAPS (Deltoid/Axillary) IIIA Resistance to Piercing and Slashing to arms/armpits Shoulders, armpits 1.5 kg 8 Throat Protector (soft) IIIA +1 AC vs neck attacks Neck 0.3 kg 5 Groin Protector (soft) IIIA +1 AC vs groin attacks Groin 0.5 kg 5 Cummerbund (soft) IIIA Resistance to Piercing and Slashing to flanks when worn with a plate carrier Flanks 0.5 kg 3

docs.title: Ballistic Shields
docs.text:
Hand-held shields for breaching and protective details. A shield grants its AC bonus only against attacks from the covered side, and occupies one hand - you cannot wield a two-handed weapon while carrying one. See Cover for how shields and ballistic blankets create cover for allies.

Shield AC Stops Modifiers Weight Req Ballistic Shield (IIIA) +3 IIIA One-handed weapons only while carried 6 kg 10 Ballistic Shield (III+) +4 III+ One-handed weapons only; -10 ft movement 10 kg 15 Breacher Shield (viewport + light) +4 III+ As III+ shield; integrated white light and armored viewport 11 kg 18 Ballistic Blanket - IIIA Deployed as an action to provide half/full cover (GM) to an adjacent prone target; no AC to wielder 7 kg 8

docs.title: Backgrounds
docs.text:
Essential - choose one during Character Creation. Your background grants one Level 1 Training Program fixed by the choice, plus a small narrative feature.

The narrative features below are written as soft guidance - the GM decides how they cash out in play. Multiple operators at the same table may share a background.

docs.title: Airborne / Paratrooper
docs.text:
You jumped out of perfectly good aircraft for a living.

  • Obstacle Course Training Level 1.
  • Jump-qualified. You take no damage from falls of 20 ft or less onto clear ground.

docs.title: Combat Diver
docs.text:
Long swims, cold water, and rebreathers.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Athletics).
  • You hold your breath twice as long as normal and are trained on rebreathers and underwater navigation.

docs.title: Combat Engineer / Sapper
docs.text:
You built bridges, breached walls, and dropped buildings.

  • Ordnance Training Level 1.
  • You read structural load and demolition needs without a check and recognize most military demo kits.

docs.title: Counter-Cyber Operator
docs.text:
Defensive cyber crew on a military network - the blue team with a chain of command.

  • Counter Ambush Training Level 1.
  • You defend military networks against intrusion and are fluent in OPSEC, COMSEC, and classification handling.

docs.title: Counter-UAS Operator
docs.text:
The other side of the sky - spotting and defeating somebody else's drone.

  • Radio Operations Training Level 1.
  • You identify drone models by sight or RF signature and are trained on jammers, net-gun systems, and directed-fire intercept doctrine.

docs.title: EOD Bomb Tech
docs.text:
The wire-cutting side of the explosive business.

  • Ordnance Training Level 1.
  • You identify commercial and military explosives by signature, and the GM may narrate the workings of an IED to you without a check.

docs.title: Foreign Legion
docs.text:
You served under a name that was not yours, for a country that was not either.

  • Fortitude Training Level 1.
  • You carry a passport of convenience and have a thick skin for abuse. Legionnaires recognize one another without a word.

docs.title: Helicopter Pilot
docs.text:
Rotary wing - nap-of-the-earth, confined landings, and a very loud commute.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Perception).
  • Rated on multi-role rotary aircraft. You read terrain and wind for landing zones without a check.

docs.title: Humanitarian Deminer
docs.text:
Non-military mine and UXO clearance - quiet, slow, and respected.

  • Counter Ambush Training Level 1.
  • You clear legacy minefields and unexploded ordnance for NGOs. Respected by local populations and by relief agencies.

docs.title: Infantry Soldier
docs.text:
You came up through the line infantry - ruck, rifle, and a lot of walking.

  • Weight Training Level 1.
  • You recognize unit insignia, rank, and standard-issue kit of regular armies at a glance.

docs.title: JTAC / Forward Observer
docs.text:
Radio, range card, and the authority to bring the sky down.

  • Radio Operations Training Level 1.
  • Trained to call and deconflict close air support, artillery, and naval fires. You know the brevity codes and risk-estimate procedures.

docs.title: Marine / Naval Infantry
docs.text:
Your war was shipboards, boardings, and beach assaults.

  • Fortitude Training Level 1.
  • You are comfortable aboard ships, small boats, and oil rigs, and you are never seasick.

docs.title: Mercenary / PMC Contractor
docs.text:
Offensive contracts, questionable clients, and a reputation that follows you.

  • Unarmed Training Level 1.
  • Prior offensive PMC experience. One old handler's phone number and a complicated reputation in the industry.

docs.title: Military Police
docs.text:
Gates, prisoners, and uncomfortable paperwork.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Insight).
  • You know arrest, detention, and chain-of-custody procedure, and can usually bluff your way through a friendly checkpoint.

docs.title: Military UAS Operator
docs.text:
Reaper, Bayraktar, or Orlan - you pulled the trigger from a trailer.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Perception).
  • Rated on military-class UAS. You are trained on the full targeting and ISR workflow, including sensor operation and strike deconfliction.

docs.title: Pararescue / CSAR
docs.text:
Combat search and rescue - you went in to bring people out.

  • Combat Life Savers Certified.
  • Trained on hostile-environment extraction including helicopter hoist rescue. You are rated as a medical jumper and swimmer.

docs.title: Prior Combat Medic
docs.text:
You pushed plasma in a gunfight before you were ever assigned to this unit.

  • Combat Life Savers Certified.
  • You know civilian hospital workflow. Once per day, you stabilize a dying NPC without a check.

docs.title: Psyops / MISO
docs.text:
Leaflets, broadcasts, and the long game for hearts and minds.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • Trained in leaflet, broadcast, and social-media influence at operational level. You know how to shape a local population around a target or message.

docs.title: Scout Sniper
docs.text:
Two-man teams, range cards, and long waits.

  • Long Range Marksmanship Level 1.
  • On hearing a shot, you can identify the weapon, caliber, and approximate range of the shooter.

docs.title: Special Forces (Unconventional Warfare)
docs.text:
You spent more time training foreign forces than firing a shot.

  • Leadership Training Level 1.
  • One sleeper contact in a host-nation militia, resistance cell, or partner unit of the GM's choice.

docs.title: Submariner
docs.text:
Long patrols, bad air, and the fine art of being quiet.

  • Resolve Training Level 1.
  • Comfortable with long durations in confined quarters. You read a crew's rhythms and can tell when morale is about to crack.

docs.title: Tank Crew / Armor Crewman
docs.text:
Steel walls, hot interiors, and a lot of coaxial rounds.

  • Fortitude Training Level 1.
  • Comfortable in armored vehicles. You identify AFVs on sight and know how to operate crew-served guns.

docs.title: UN Peacekeeper
docs.text:
Blue helmet, white truck, impossible rules of engagement.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • Recognized by UN and NGO personnel in the field. You can request shelter, medical care, or a phone at any UN or recognized NGO compound.

docs.title: Case Officer (Field Operative)
docs.text:
Hand-shakes, dead drops, and careful lies.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • Two cultivated contacts in two different cities of your choice.

docs.title: Counterintelligence Officer
docs.text:
You spent your career spotting the spies in your own building.

  • Counter Ambush Training Level 1.
  • Advantage on checks to detect physical or electronic surveillance targeting you or your team.

docs.title: Crossover Analyst (Desk to Door)
docs.text:
You spent years reading the targets before someone put a kit on you and sent you to meet them.

  • Advanced Awareness Training Level 1.
  • You keep informal ties in the analytical cells. Once per mission, a former colleague will read you a short pattern-of-life summary on a named individual or location over the phone.

docs.title: Cyber Operator
docs.text:
Network implants, endpoint compromise, and a laptop bag that never leaves your side.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Technology).
  • You recognize common OS and network fingerprints, identify obvious malware families at a glance, and can usually bluff basic sysadmin or help-desk roles to get physical or network access.

docs.title: Deep Cover Officer (NOC)
docs.text:
No diplomatic passport, no embassy to run to - just a legend and nerve.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • You maintain a complete legend (papers, address, employment history, banking) in one country of your choice. It holds up to a routine police check.

docs.title: Disinformation / Influence Operator
docs.text:
Runs personas, shapes narratives, and reads the algorithm like a weather report.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • You build and run a persona at scale on social platforms, and can recognize an ongoing influence campaign from the outside.

docs.title: Imagery / GEOINT Analyst
docs.text:
Overhead pictures, contour lines, and a lot of squinting at pixels.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Perception).
  • You identify vehicle types, unit sizes, and construction use from satellite or drone imagery, and can read a contour map at speed.

docs.title: Intelligence Analyst (Desk)
docs.text:
Your war was fluorescent lighting and source files.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Investigation).
  • Once per downtime, you can request one document or dossier from the unit's archive.

docs.title: Interrogator / HUMINT Collector
docs.text:
Booth lights, stale coffee, and long silences.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Insight).
  • When questioning a subject under interview conditions, you spot lies on a passive check. Trained on detention procedure and EPW handling.

docs.title: OSINT Analyst
docs.text:
Open-source work - databases, social media, and a patient browser.

  • Education Training Level 1.
  • Given a few hours and an internet connection, you can build a usable dossier on any public figure without tipping them off.

docs.title: SIGINT Operator
docs.text:
Antennas, intercepts, and headphones in a darkened van.

  • Radio Operations Training Level 1.
  • You recognize traffic patterns and encryption styles, can usually identify the service branch behind a transmission, and are familiar with direction finding.

docs.title: Surveillance Operator (Watcher)
docs.text:
Two cars, three foot teams, and a comms net.

  • Covert Movement Training Level 1.
  • You shadow a target through an urban environment without being made, and can spot a surveillance team tailing someone else.

docs.title: Technical Operations Officer
docs.text:
Pinhole cameras, line-taps, and the thin end of a pick.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Sleight of Hand).
  • You plant and retrieve covert recording devices in occupied spaces and are familiar with common commercial lock types.

docs.title: Threat Intelligence Analyst
docs.text:
You spent your days tracking APT groups and arguing about attribution.

  • Education Training Level 1.
  • You attribute an intrusion to a likely threat actor from observed tradecraft, and maintain a personal source list in the threat-research community.

docs.title: Coast Guard / Harbor Patrol
docs.text:
Boardings, SAR, and very long night shifts on the water.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Athletics).
  • Rated on SAR and small-boat boardings. Trained on anti-smuggling inspection; you can drive most small vessels.

docs.title: Corrections Officer
docs.text:
Tiers, gates, and a yard that reads like a chessboard.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Insight).
  • You read violent body language before it moves. You know the gang politics inside the prisons of your home country.

docs.title: Customs / Border Agent
docs.text:
Bags, passports, and lies about declared value.

  • Advanced Awareness Training Level 1.
  • You passively spot false papers, hidden compartments, and contraband.

docs.title: Cybercrime Investigator
docs.text:
FBI Cyber, BKA CC, or the national equivalent - a badge with a keyboard.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Investigation).
  • You coordinate with ISPs, payment providers, and exchanges under subpoena, and are familiar with crypto tracing and dark-market takedowns.

docs.title: Detective
docs.text:
Case files, interviews, and three coffees before lunch.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Investigation).
  • Once per mission, an old colleague reads a relevant case file to you over the phone.

docs.title: Federal Agent
docs.text:
A three-letter badge and a lot of jurisdictional headaches.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Intimidation).
  • You carry a now-expired federal badge. NPCs in your home country tend to assume authority until they verify it.

docs.title: Forensic Technician (CSI)
docs.text:
Latex gloves, evidence bags, and the long drive to the lab.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Investigation).
  • You read and preserve a crime scene without contaminating it, and know what will and will not hold up in evidentiary procedure.

docs.title: Internal Affairs
docs.text:
The cop the other cops dislike.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • Trained to interview fellow officers under pressure. You are immune to the usual LE professional-courtesy bluffs.

docs.title: Patrol Officer (Beat Cop)
docs.text:
A badge, a radio, and a lot of night shifts.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Insight).
  • You have one ex-partner still on the job in your home city, and you know that city's criminal geography block by block.

docs.title: Police K-9 Handler
docs.text:
You and your dog worked the city together - patrol, detection, and the occasional bite.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Animal Handling).
  • Trained on patrol and detection dogs. You know the K-9 deployment workflow and can read what a working dog is telling you without prompting.

docs.title: SWAT Operator
docs.text:
Stack, breach, clear.

  • Close Quarters Battle (CQB) Training Level 1.
  • You are trained on dynamic breaching and know the hostage-negotiation playbook from the entry side.

docs.title: Wildlife Ranger / Park Warden
docs.text:
Back-country patrol, poaching cases, and long hours on a radio.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Survival).
  • You know reserve patrol routines and poacher TTPs. Inside a recognized park or reserve, your authority is respected by local law enforcement.

docs.title: Academic
docs.text:
Before the war pulled you in, you were on a tenure track.

  • Education Training Level 1.
  • Pick a field (history, engineering, linguistics, biology, and so on). You are treated as an expert in that field by peers and laypeople alike.

docs.title: Bartender
docs.text:
Pour, listen, nod, repeat.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • You read a room on a passive check. In a city where you have worked, at least one regular will vouch for you.

docs.title: Bush Pilot
docs.text:
Grass strips, crosswinds, and freight no one else will fly.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Nature).
  • Rated on STOL aircraft. You can land or take off on unprepared strips of roughly 200 m or longer.

docs.title: Chaplain
docs.text:
A collar, a flask, and a steady voice for bad days.

  • Resolve Training Level 1.
  • Respected across faiths. You can usually request shelter or information at any house of worship.

docs.title: Civilian Paramedic
docs.text:
Before the unit, you rode an ambulance.

  • Combat Life Savers Certified.
  • You know hospital triage workflow and can usually bluff staff status at any ER in your home country.

docs.title: Combat Photographer
docs.text:
Like the journalist, but with more time behind a lens and less behind a keyboard.

  • Advanced Awareness Training Level 1.
  • You own a personal camera kit and can produce publishable photographs. You have one stringer or wire-service contact.

docs.title: Combat-sports Athlete
docs.text:
Boxing, wrestling, or MMA - you have a record to your name.

  • Unarmed Training Level 1.
  • Minor name recognition in your home country's combat-sports community.

docs.title: Commercial Drone Pilot
docs.text:
Aerial survey, inspection, agriculture - you flew for a living.

  • Education Training Level 1.
  • Rated on commercial drone classes. You can plan and fly autonomous survey missions and produce usable mapping output.

docs.title: Commercial Fisherman
docs.text:
Cold mornings, wet boots, and a knife that stays sharp.

  • Cardio Training Level 1.
  • Sea legs, fillet-knife skills, and comfort on small vessels in rough water. You read weather without a check.

docs.title: Cook / Line Chef
docs.text:
Kitchens run on the same chain of command that units do.

  • Field Mess.
  • A hot meal you prepare at a halt visibly lifts morale. The GM may grant narrative dividends (looser tongues, better rest) at their discretion.

docs.title: Cult Escapee
docs.text:
You were in, you are out, and you do not like to talk about it.

  • Resolve Training Level 1.
  • You know high-control-group manipulation from the inside. You recognize coercive tactics used against yourself or others without a check.

docs.title: Dog Groomer
docs.text:
Clippers, a bathtub, and half the neighborhood's dogs on a first-name basis.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • You can approach and calm almost any domestic animal. Through your clients you know whose dog lives where in a chosen neighborhood.

docs.title: Drone Engineer / Builder
docs.text:
You designed and soldered the things other people fly.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Technology).
  • You field-repair downed drones from scrap, and can modify a commercial drone to carry a small payload or extend its range.

docs.title: Electrician
docs.text:
Conduit, breakers, and a healthy respect for 400 volts.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Technology).
  • You read building electrical systems at a glance and can safely bypass or disable most alarm wiring.

docs.title: Ex-Criminal / Smuggler
docs.text:
You moved goods and people across borders that were not yours.

  • Covert Movement Training Level 1.
  • One underworld contact, and knowledge of one black-market route across a border of your choice.

docs.title: Exploit Developer / Vulnerability Researcher
docs.text:
Finds zero-days for a living, for love or for money.

  • Education Training Level 1.
  • You read assembly, write shellcode, and understand low-level OS internals without a check.

docs.title: Falconer
docs.text:
Hood, glove, and a bird that knows its way home.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Animal Handling).
  • You handle trained raptors. With time and line of sight, your bird can scout a rooftop or strike a small target on cue.

docs.title: Firefighter
docs.text:
Structural fires, extractions, and stair climbs in full kit.

  • Resistance Training Level 1 (fire and smoke).
  • You read building construction and likely failure points at a glance and know the escape routes before you enter.

docs.title: Fixer
docs.text:
Local hustler in a place nobody with a camera knows.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • In a conflict zone of your choice, you know who pays, who moves goods, and who will talk - and for how much.

docs.title: FPV Hobbyist / Drone Racer
docs.text:
Goggles, a self-built quad, and a garage full of broken props.

  • Agility Training Level 1.
  • You fly consumer FPV drones instinctively and can build, tune, or repair them from parts.

docs.title: Gunsmith
docs.text:
Lathe, mill, and a wall of jigs for rifles other people forgot.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Technology).
  • You fabricate and modify firearms. You identify any firearm's provenance from proof marks and serials without a check.

docs.title: Hardware Hacker / Reverse Engineer
docs.text:
Firmware, RFID, and a soldering iron that is always warm.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Sleight of Hand).
  • You clone RFID badges, solder a debug interface onto consumer hardware, and dump firmware without bricking it.

docs.title: Hunter / Outdoorsman
docs.text:
Long weekends on stands, in blinds, and on back-country trails.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Survival).
  • You field-dress game and keep a personal hunting rifle stored at a family home.

docs.title: ICS / OT Security Specialist
docs.text:
Industrial control systems - SCADA, PLCs, and the grown-up end of cybersecurity.

  • Education Training Level 1.
  • You read a plant's process diagram and identify its attack surface. You understand that crashing a PLC can crash a boiler.

docs.title: Incident Responder (DFIR)
docs.text:
Corporate breach response - logs, disk images, and a very long weekend.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Investigation).
  • You reconstruct an intrusion from logs and disk images, and preserve chain of custody for digital evidence that will hold up in court.

docs.title: Journalist
docs.text:
Ink, press passes, and questions people did not want answered.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • Press credentials (real or forged to your standard) and one newsroom contact of your choice.

docs.title: Knife Thrower / Circus Performer
docs.text:
Spotlights, tight schedules, and a very tolerant assistant.

  • Thrown Training Level 1.
  • Professional-grade accuracy with balanced throwing objects. You have one contact in a traveling show or circus.

docs.title: Linguist / Translator
docs.text:
Dictionaries, field notebooks, and an ear for how people actually talk.

  • Language Training Level 1 (a language of your choice).
  • You pick up functional survival-level conversation in any new language after a week of exposure. Credentialed for court or diplomatic translation in at least one language pair.

docs.title: Locksmith
docs.text:
Pins, wafers, and a box of tools that looks harmless in a briefcase.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Sleight of Hand).
  • You defeat most commercial locks in a minute and can produce a working key from an impression.

docs.title: Long-haul Trucker
docs.text:
Ten hours of asphalt, diesel, and truck-stop coffee.

  • Cardio Training Level 1.
  • Licensed on every civilian vehicle class. Six hours in a sleeper cab counts as a long rest for you.

docs.title: Makeup Artist
docs.text:
Film sets, theater, and the occasional bridal party - you make people look like other people.

  • Covert Movement Training Level 1.
  • Given thirty minutes and a working kit, you can disguise yourself or an ally well enough to pass casual inspection at conversational distance.

docs.title: Mechanic
docs.text:
Civilian garage or motor pool - either way, you know engines.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (tool-flavored skill of your choice).
  • You can hotwire a civilian vehicle in one minute and field-diagnose most light-vehicle faults.

docs.title: Mountaineer / Alpine Climber
docs.text:
Ropes, ice axes, and weeks above the tree line.

  • Obstacle Course Training Level 1.
  • Rated on technical rope work and glacier travel. Comfortable at altitude and in cold weather.

docs.title: Night Security Guard
docs.text:
Rounds, radio checks, and a lot of empty hallways.

  • Awareness Training Level 1.
  • You know the rhythm of empty buildings after hours. You still have a ring of keys or fobs you never gave back when you quit.

docs.title: Nightclub Bouncer
docs.text:
The door, the list, and the polite firm no.

  • Unarmed Training Level 1.
  • You de-escalate a fight before it starts and read drunk versus dangerous on a passive check. You know the local security circuit.

docs.title: Oil Rig Roughneck
docs.text:
Pipe, derricks, and shifts measured in weeks.

  • Weight Training Level 1.
  • Comfortable with heavy machinery, cranes, and welding in extreme environments.

docs.title: Private Pilot
docs.text:
Rented Cessnas, cross-country hops, and a fat logbook.

  • Education Training Level 1.
  • Rated on light fixed-wing aircraft. You have one flight-school buddy at a regional airfield of your choice.

docs.title: Private Security / Bodyguard
docs.text:
Close protection, corporate escorts, and private airfields.

  • Envelopment Training Level 1.
  • Familiar with VIP protection TTPs. One contact in a private military company of the GM's choice.

docs.title: Pyrotechnician (Stage Fireworks)
docs.text:
Licensed to put fire in the sky on cue.

  • Grenade Training Level 1.
  • You time and place small charges for effect. Licensed stage pyro in at least one country.

docs.title: Rally / Stunt Driver
docs.text:
Tarmac, gravel, and a very busy co-driver.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Athletics).
  • Rated on performance driving. You can shake a tail or lose a pursuer on wheels in conditions most drivers could not.

docs.title: Ranch Hand / Horse Trainer
docs.text:
Saddles, fencing, and long days with large stubborn animals.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Animal Handling).
  • You can ride or pack any domestic mount, rope and work cattle, and are comfortable on rural spreads.

docs.title: Red Team Operator / Pentester
docs.text:
Authorized offensive security - paid to get in and write it up.

  • Covert Movement Training Level 1.
  • Trained on badge-cloning, tailgating, and common corporate network weaknesses. You are comfortable on both sides of the keyboard and the door.

docs.title: Ship's Engineer
docs.text:
Greasy overalls, ringing ears, and a wrench longer than your forearm.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (tool-flavored skill of your choice).
  • You are familiar with marine engines, shipboard power plants, welding, and industrial plumbing.

docs.title: SOC Analyst
docs.text:
Security Operations Center - alerts, feeds, and a pager that owned your nights.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Technology).
  • You triage a stream of alerts and pull the likely-true-positive from the noise. Fluent in SIEM and EDR tooling.

docs.title: Social Engineer
docs.text:
Pretexting, vishing, and in-person cons - the human exploit.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • You talk past a receptionist, help desk, or security guard with a plausible pretext on a passive check.

docs.title: Stage Magician / Illusionist
docs.text:
Patter, flash paper, and a deck that never does what it looks like.

  • Ambush Training Level 1.
  • Professional misdirection at parlor distance. You palm, switch, or plant small objects without being seen.

docs.title: Street Performer / Busker
docs.text:
A guitar case, a crowd, and the daily hustle for coins.

  • Social Training Level 1.
  • You command a crowd's attention on cue. You know a dozen small-money hustles in any city you have worked.

docs.title: Stunt Performer
docs.text:
Falls, fires, and very expensive insurance.

  • Evasion Training Level 1.
  • Choreographed falls, car crashes, and stage fires. A union card in film work and one stunt-coordinator contact.

docs.title: Survivalist / Prepper
docs.text:
Canned food, a rural cabin, and a very long list of what-ifs.

  • Physical Skills Training Level 1 (Survival).
  • You maintain a rural cache of food, tools, and firearms. One contact in the broader paranoid-networks community.

docs.title: Taxi / Rideshare Driver
docs.text:
Ten-hour shifts, every neighborhood, every kind of fare.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Perception).
  • You carry a map of one city in your head. You read a fare before they speak.

docs.title: Third-Culture Kid / Diplomat's Child
docs.text:
You grew up in airports and on the wrong side of the expat compound wall.

  • Language Training Level 1 (a language of your choice).
  • You read social registers others miss - host-country manners, expatriate cliques, and class markers in three countries.

docs.title: Veterinarian
docs.text:
Full vet school, plus a rural practice that taught you to improvise.

  • Education Training Level 1.
  • You stabilize wounded animals and diagnose livestock or pets without a check. You are credible at rural farms and with animal-rescue NGOs.

docs.title: War Refugee
docs.text:
You survived what your hometown did not.

  • Resolve Training Level 1.
  • You know the logistics of flight - papers, trains, borders, and who to avoid. One contact in an émigré community of your choice.

docs.title: Zookeeper / Exotic Handler
docs.text:
Lions, tigers, tranquilizer rifles, and the odd snake bite.

  • Mental Skills Training Level 1 (Animal Handling).
  • You are comfortable with large or dangerous wildlife and are trained on tranquilizer dosing and containment procedure.

docs.title: Character Creation
docs.text:
This page walks you through building a 1st-level Operator from top to bottom. Follow the steps in order. Each section tells you what to decide and links to the page with the full rules. When you reach the checklist at the end, your character sheet should be ready for play.

If your table is starting above 1st level, complete every step below for level 1 first, then level the character up using the Operator Class table and pick a Combat Designation at 3rd level.

docs.title: Step 1 - Concept and Name
docs.text:
Before you touch any numbers, sketch the operator in your head. Answer these in a sentence or two each:

  • Name and callsign. A legal name for paperwork and a callsign the squad actually uses on comms.
  • Background. Which service did they come from - Army, Navy, Marines, Air Force, police SWAT, a foreign ally? What put them on the unit's radar? You will formally pick a Background for its mechanical effect in Step 4, but it helps to have one in mind already.
  • Appearance and age. Operators skew late 20s to mid 40s. Anything visible when kitted up (scars, tattoos, build)?
  • Hook. One line on what drives them - a debt, a mentor, a mission that never closed.

None of this costs points. It shapes the picks you make below.

Example: Kevin Becker, callsign "Falke" - a 32-year-old former Fallschirmjäger sergeant from the Bundeswehr, lean build, faint burn scar along the right jaw. Hook: his previous team leader was killed on a joint op in Mali on bad intel, and Kevin signed on to the unit to chase the people who cooked the brief.

docs.title: Step 2 - Attributes
docs.text:
Your character's most basic strengths and weaknesses are defined by six attributes - Strength (STR), Dexterity (DEX), Constitution (CON), Intelligence (INT), Wisdom (WIS), and Charisma (CHA) - each of which begins with a score of 8 to 18.

Your character's attribute scores begin at 8 each. You may spend 36 points to increase these scores, as shown in the table below.

Assign the highest scores to the attributes that represent your operator's strengths and the lowest scores to the ones that represent their weaknesses. Your Personality Trait (next step) will adjust one or more of them, so leave a little room.

Score Cost 8 0 9 1 10 2 11 3 12 4 13 6 14 8 15 11 16 14 17 18 18 22

Example: Kevin uses his 36 points to purchase a Strength of 13 (6 points), a Dexterity of 15 (11 points), a Constitution of 11 (3 points), an Intelligence of 12 (4 points), a Wisdom of 12 (4 points), and a Charisma of 14 (8 points).

docs.title: Step 3 - Personality Trait
docs.text:
See Personality Traits and pick one. This is the equivalent of your Race pick in classic D&D 5E. Apply its attribute adjustments to the scores from Step 2, and note any proficiencies or special bonuses on your sheet.

Example: Kevin picks Agile (+2 DEX, -2 STR, +5 ft movement, Proficiency in Acrobatics). His attributes become STR 11, DEX 17, CON 11, INT 12, WIS 12, CHA 14. He notes Acrobatics proficiency and the movement bonus.

docs.title: Step 4 - Background
docs.text:
Pick a Background. Your background grants one Level 1 Training Program fixed by the choice, plus a small narrative feature.

Example: Kevin picks Airborne / Paratrooper, reflecting his Fallschirmjäger service. He gains Obstacle Course Training Level 1 and is jump-qualified (no damage from falls of 20 ft or less onto clear ground).

docs.title: Step 5 - Nationality / Languages
docs.text:
Decide your operator's home country and primary language, then allocate Language Training. Pick one of the following:

  • Language Training Level 1-4 in English, or
  • Language Training Level 1-3 in English plus Level 1 in another language of your choice, or
  • Language Training Level 1-4 in a non-English mother tongue plus Level 1-2 in English (NATO working level).

See Language Training for what each level covers.

Example: Kevin is German, so he takes the third option: Level 1-4 in German (fluent, mother tongue) plus Level 1-2 in English (NATO working level).

docs.title: Step 6 - Operator Class
docs.text: Every player character in Modern Warfare 5E is an Operator. The full progression lives in Operator Class; at 1st level, work through each sub-step below.

docs.title: 6a. Hit Points
docs.text:
Your maximum hit points at 1st level equal 10 + your Constitution score (the full score, not the modifier).

Example: Kevin has CON 11, so his HP is 10 + 11 = 21.

docs.title: 6b. Basic Training - pick one style
docs.text:
Pick one fighting style from Basic Training. You gain another later when you level-up on levels 10 and 20.

Example: Kevin picks Marksman (+2 to attack rolls with ranged weapons), leaning into his paratrooper rifle background.

docs.title: 6c. Second Wind
docs.text:
No choice to make here - you have Second Wind from 1st level. Note it on your sheet; see Second Wind.

Example: Kevin writes "Second Wind: bonus action, regain 1d10 + level HP, once per short rest" on his sheet.

docs.title: 6d. Starter Training Programs
docs.text:
No choice to make here - you receive all of the following for free with the class:

  • Armor Training Level 1 in Light and Medium.
  • Weapons Training Level 1 in Assault Rifles, Pistols, Grenades, Grenade Launchers, Combat Knife, and Bayonet.
  • Saving Throw Training Level 1 in Strength and Dexterity.

Example: Kevin records all three blocks as granted. No picks needed.

docs.title: 6e. Skills - pick 2 + INT modifier
docs.text:
Pick 2 + your Intelligence modifier skills (minimum 2) from this list, each at Level 1: Acrobatics, Animal Handling, Athletics, History, Insight, Intimidation, Investigation, Perception, Nature, Survival.

Example: Kevin has INT 12 (+1 modifier), so he picks 3 skills: Perception, Athletics, and Survival.

docs.title: Step 7 - Equipment
docs.text:
Your starting loadout has three parts:

  1. Standard Issue gear package - granted free. See Field Kit for exactly what's in it.
  2. 30 Requisition Points for Weapons. Spend from Weapons. Each weapon comes with a baseline pool of magazines free; see Ammunition. Unspent points are lost.
  3. 20 Requisition Points for Armor. Spend from Armor. Unspent points are lost.

Prioritize weapons and armor you are already trained in (see the Starter Training Programs above). You can carry gear you aren't trained in, but you won't get the bonuses and may have disadvantage.

Example: Kevin takes his Standard Issue kit, then spends all 30 Weapon Req on an assault rifle with a basic optic plus a sidearm, and all 20 Armor Req on a medium flak vest with front/back plates.

docs.title: Step 8 - Combat Designation (only if starting at 3<sup>rd</sup> level or higher)
docs.text:
At 3rd level an Operator locks in a Combat Designation - Rifleman, Marksman, Machine Gunner, Demolitions Specialist, Unit Leader, Combat Medic, Dog Handler, or Tech Specialist. Apply its 3rd-level feature, plus any 7th, 10th, 15th, or 18th-level features you qualify for at your starting level. At 10th level you also pick a specialization path within the designation.

Example (starting at 3rd level): Kevin picks Marksman as his designation, doubling down on his Fallschirmjäger rifle habit. He records the proficiencies:

  • Weapon Training Level 1 in Bolt Action Rifles and Single Shot Rifles.
  • Save Training Level 1 in Wisdom Saves.
  • Perception and Stealth training (Level 1).
  • Observation Kit proficiency (spotting scope, rifle optic, laser rangefinder, wind meter, DOPE book).

And the 3rd-level feature: Critical Shot - as an action, fire one shot from a bolt-action or single-shot rifle; if it hits, it auto-crits. If the underlying roll is a natural 20, the damage dice triple instead of double.

With the designation's weapon training stacked on top of his Basic Training (also Marksman, +2 to ranged attack rolls - note the name collision), his DEX 17 (+3), and his Proficiency Bonus +2 at level 3, Kevin's to-hit with a bolt-action rifle is +7 (+2 Proficiency Bonus, +3 DEX, +2 Basic Training). Critical Shot on top of that makes a landed hit automatically a crit.

docs.title: Step 9 - Final Pass
docs.text:
Before the first session, sanity-check the sheet:

  • Recompute AC using your armor + any trait bonuses.
  • Recompute movement using base speed + any trait or Basic Training bonuses.
  • Note your Proficiency Bonus from the Operator Class table (+2 at levels 1-4).
  • Write down what Second Wind, Action Surge, and any designation features actually do, so you don't stall in combat.

Example: Kevin totals his sheet: HP 21, AC from medium flak with plates, movement 35 ft (30 base + 5 from Agile), Proficiency Bonus +2, Second Wind ready. Falke is cleared hot.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• Name, callsign, and short background written down
  • 36 attribute points spent; all six attributes recorded
  • Personality Trait picked and its adjustments applied
  • Background picked; its Level 1 Training Program and narrative feature noted
  • Nationality set and Language Training allocation chosen
  • Hit points = 10 + Constitution score
  • Basic Training fighting style chosen
  • Starter Training Programs (Armor, Weapons, Saves) noted on sheet
  • Skills picked (2 + INT modifier from the class list)
  • Standard Issue gear recorded
  • 30 Req spent on Weapons
  • 20 Req spent on Armor
  • AC and movement recomputed after gear and traits
  • Proficiency Bonus noted (+2 at levels 1-4)
  • Second Wind noted; Action Surge at 2+, Indomitable at 9+, Resourceful at 19 as applicable
  • (3rd level+) Combat Designation picked and its features applied

docs.title: Combat Designations
docs.text:
At 3rd level, an Operator chooses a Combat Designation. Each grants features at 3rd, 7th, 10th, 15th, and 18th level. See the Operator Class for level progression.

  • Rifleman
  • Machine Gunner
  • Marksman
  • Demolitions Specialist
  • Unit Leader
  • Combat Medic
  • Dog Handler
  • Tech Specialist

docs.title: Specialization Paths
docs.text:
At 10th level, each designation branches into a specialization. See the individual designation pages for mechanics.

Combat Designations
├── Rifleman
│   ├── Assaulter
│   ├── Breacher
│   └── Grenadier
├── Machine Gunner
│   ├── Suppressor
│   └── Gun Hand
├── Marksman
│   ├── Sharpshooter
│   ├── Pathfinder
│   └── Counter-Sniper
├── Demolitions Specialist
│   ├── Demolitionist
│   ├── Combat Engineer
│   └── AT Specialist
├── Unit Leader
│   ├── NCO
│   ├── Tactician
│   └── Liaison
├── Combat Medic
│   ├── Trauma Specialist
│   ├── Combat Pharmacist
│   └── CBRN Specialist
├── Dog Handler
│   ├── Tactical Handler
│   └── Kennel Master
└── Tech Specialist
    ├── Drone Operator
    ├── Signals Specialist
    └── Hacker

docs.title: Gear
docs.text: Clothing, packs, field kit, comms, optics, electronics, medical, demolitions, and drones. Item effects are primarily narrative - each item carries tags that the GM uses to rule on situational advantage, access, or capability. Weight values are provided so Operators can track encumbrance.

docs.title: Gear Tags
docs.text: Axis Tag Effect Vision night-vision Treat darkness/dim light as bright light out to device range (typ. 100 ft). Vision thermal See heat signatures through smoke, foliage, and light concealment. Vision magnified Advantage on Perception (sight) at long range; disadvantage to spot close movement. Signal encrypted Comms cannot be intercepted or read by unequipped parties. Signal long-range Effective beyond standard squad comms (regional/sat coverage). Signal short-range Line-of-sight only; small radius (typ. under 1 km). Signal jams-comms Suppresses enemy radio traffic in a short radius. Detection detects-motion Alerts to movement in a defined area. Detection detects-rf Picks up radio or electronic emissions in range. Utility breaching Can defeat locks, doors, walls, or similar obstacles. Utility lockpicks Enables Sleight of Hand / Thieves' Tools checks vs mechanical locks. Utility repair Enables repair checks on a specific class of equipment (drone, weapon, vehicle). Medical stops-bleed Stabilizes a bleeding target; interacts with Triage Code. Medical revives Brings an unconscious target back to Triage Code Yellow at GM discretion. Carry covert Conceals contents from casual inspection (civilian blend). Carry high-capacity Holds substantially more than a standard pack; incurs weight.

docs.title: Mission Kits
docs.text:
All-in-one bundles that answer "what's the op?" - grab a row for the mission type. The sections linked below break each kit into individual pieces.

Kit Weight Role Contents (summary) Direct Action ~18 kg Offensive raid / HVT action Main ruck + IFAK + NVG + encrypted squad radio + breaching charge + extra mags Reconnaissance ~12 kg Long-range stealth observation Day pack + binoculars + spotter scope + radio + GPS + light survival kit Long-Range Patrol ~25 kg Multi-day overland insertion Full ruck + sat radio + NVG + GPS + rations + survival kit + water filter Hostage Rescue / CQB ~15 kg Building clear, breach-and-rescue Chest rig + IFAK + NVG + flashbangs + breaching charge + encrypted radio Protective Detail ~6 kg Civilian escort / low-profile security Covert pouch + concealable IFAK + earpiece + short-range radio Humanitarian / SERE ~10 kg Non-combat or escape-and-evasion Day pack + expanded medical + radio + GPS + survival kit

docs.title: Designation Kits
docs.text:
The tool proficiency granted by each Combat Designation comes bundled as an all-in-one pack. Add the row matching your designation to your inventory instead of tracking the individual pieces.

Kit Weight Designation Contents (summary) LBS Kit ~2.5 kg Rifleman Modular MOLLE chest rig or load-bearing vest with mag, admin, grenade, IFAK, and radio pouches (empty weight). T&E Kit ~2.0 kg Machine Gunner Traverse and Elevation mechanism for tripod-mounted MGs; dial-and-scale head, clamp bracket, T&E plate. Observation Kit ~2.0 kg Marksman Spotting scope with field tripod, laser rangefinder, wind meter, DOPE book, and range cards. Demolitions Kit ~3.0 kg Demolitions Specialist Electric and non-electric caps, time fuse, det cord, crimpers, clacker, galvanometer, wire, tape, EOD probes. Command Kit ~3.0 kg Unit Leader Map case with laminated sheets, compass, protractor, range cards, tactical radio, and binoculars. Medic's Kit ~2.0 kg Combat Medic Stethoscope, BP cuff, pulse oximeter, airways, BVM, decompression needle, suture kit, IV gear, syringes. Handler's Kit ~3.5 kg Dog Handler Working lead, long tracking line, tactical harness and muzzle, bite sleeve, clicker and whistle, e-collar, reward pouch, canine IFAK. B1ARD Kit ~2.0 kg Tech Specialist Diagnostic unit, spare rotors and optics, micro-tools, firmware loader, and replacement battery pack.

docs.title: Gear Index
docs.text:
• Clothing - civilian, military, and tactical apparel, including protective gloves and boots.
  • Packs - rucksacks, assault packs, chest rigs, battle belts, pouches.
  • Field Kit - standard-issue items, canteens, sleeping bags, multi-tools, flashlights.
  • Consumables - rations, drinks, stimulants, vices, and cooking fuel.
  • Comms - walkie-talkies, squad and sat radios, earpieces, crypto keys.
  • Optics - night-vision, thermal, binoculars, monoculars, rangefinders, spotters.
  • Electronics - jammers, scanners, bugs, sensors, IR strobes, lock-pick sets.
  • Medical - IFAK, trauma bags, tourniquets, hemostatic agents, stims.
  • Demolitions - C4, det cord, blasting caps, clackers, breaching charges.
  • Drones - B-1 Recon Drone, Black Hornet, loitering munitions.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
Alphabetical index of topics, terms, and mechanics covered by the compendium. Each entry links to the page (or pages) where the topic is defined. For a list of short-form abbreviations and unit symbols, see Abbreviations.

  • Ability Checks - See Ability Checks.
  • Ammunition - See Ammunition.
  • Action - See Actions, Combat Actions.
  • Action Surge - See Action Surge, Combat Turn.
  • Advantage and Disadvantage - See Advantage.
  • Aim (action) - See Aim.
  • Armor - See Armor.
  • Armor Class (AC) - See Armor.
  • Assault Rifles - See Assault Rifles.
  • Attack Action - See Actions, Combat Actions.
  • Attributes - See Attributes.
  • Automatic Fire - See Automatic Firing Modes, Weapon Properties.
  • Backgrounds - See Backgrounds.
  • Ballistic Shields - See Ballistic Shields.
  • Basic Training - See Basic Training.
  • Bleed - See Bleed, Major Bleed.
  • Bolt-Action Rifles - See Bolt-Action Rifles.
  • Bonus Action - See Bonus Actions.
  • Breaking Contact - See Breaking Contact.
  • Burst Fire - See Burst Fire.
  • Carry Limits - See Carry Limits, Encumbrance.
  • Character Creation - See Character Creation.
  • Charisma (CHA) - See Attributes.
  • Climbing - See Climbing, Swimming, Crawling.
  • Clothing - See Clothing.
  • Combat Actions - See Combat Actions.
